params.text:
Supporting good mental wellbeing

Good mental health is not just the absence of a condition. It grows from the everyday things that give life meaning: connection, purpose, activity and a sense of control. As a care worker you are well placed to nurture these in small, consistent ways.

  • Connection: a warm greeting, time to talk, and help to stay in touch with family and friends.
  • Purpose: chances to contribute, whether folding laundry, tending a plant or choosing the day's music.
  • Activity: movement, fresh air and hobbies that suit the person's interests and abilities.
  • Control: real choices over daily life, from what to wear to when to rise.

Small things, real difference

Wellbeing is built through relationships and routine, not grand gestures. When you take the time to learn what matters to a person and weave it into their day, you protect their sense of identity and belonging.

Notice what lifts someone and what drains them. A favourite radio programme, a walk in the garden, or a chat about grandchildren can steady a difficult day. Equally, rushing, noise or being talked over can wear a person down.

  • Offer choice, not commands: “Would you like tea or coffee?” respects autonomy.
  • Protect dignity: privacy, gentle language and unhurried care all matter.
  • Encourage strengths: focus on what a person can do, not only what they cannot.

Promoting wellbeing sits alongside spotting problems. Both keep the person, not the task, at the centre of your care.

params.question: Promoting positive mental health means only stepping in once a person shows signs of a mental health condition.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Supporting wellbeing is proactive and ongoing, not just a reaction to signs of a condition.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Wellbeing is built every day through connection, purpose, activity and choice — not only in response to problems.
